content.text: Based on the official Claude Code documentation and expert resources,
event-driven architecture where AI agents automatically execute at predefined times
using cron-like scheduling, enabling regular automation without manual prompting or babysitting.

attrs.alt: Cron/scheduled agents (Claude Scheduled Tasks pattern) concept diagram

content.text: — set a task once, it executes automatically at specific times with no prompting needed, similar to cron jobs for Claude Code.

One-time reminder: "run at 3pm today"
    ↓
Recurring task: "every day at 9am"
    ↓
Cron expression: "0 9 * * 1-5" (weekdays at 9am)
    ↓
Task pins fire time → Executes automatically → Deletes itself (one-time)
: Loops are "cron for Claude Code" but not the same as Scheduled Tasks — loops run continuously, scheduled tasks run at specific times.

User schedules task (natural language or cron)
    ↓
Claude pins fire time (specific minute/hour)
    ↓
Task stored in scheduler
    ↓
At scheduled time: OS scheduler triggers task
    ↓
Task executes automatically
    ↓
Task deletes itself (one-time) or continues (recurring)
Run once at a specific time,

Examples:
- "run at 3pm today"
- "tomorrow at noon"
- "next Tuesday at 2pm"
: Unless "every", "daily", "weekly", or similar recurring keywords present → treated as

Examples:
- "every day at 9am"
- "daily at 6pm"
- "weekdays at 10am"
- Cron: "0 9 * * 1-5"
(second, minute, hour, day, month, weekday, year):

"every weekday at 9am" → "0 9 * * 1-5"
"tomorrow at noon" → one-time task
"daily at 6pm" → recurring task
Tasks are executed by your OS's native scheduler:

Scheduled time arrives
    ↓
OS scheduler triggers task
    ↓
Claude Code executes prompt
    ↓
Prompt can:
- Create/update files [web:223]
- Access skills and tools [web:223]
- Use browser extension [web:223]
: Like anything in Cowork, can access files/folders, skills, and tools

Schedule: "every day at 9am"
Prompt: "Generate daily analytics report from data directory"
Result: Automated report generation without manual prompting

Schedule: "every 15 minutes"
Prompt: "Check server health and alert if issues detected"
Result: Continuous monitoring without babysitting

Schedule: "every day at 6pm"
Prompt: "Backup database to external storage"
Result: Regular backups without manual intervention

Schedule: "weekdays at 10am"
Prompt: "Generate weekly project summary"
Result: Automated status updates

Schedule: "next Tuesday at 2pm"
Prompt: "Review project milestone and send reminder"
Result: One-time reminder, auto-deletes after execution
CLI is in PATH for scheduler
Check scheduler health, verify task enabled
Ensure claude CLI has execute permissions
setting an alarm clock for automation

: you tell Claude what to do and when to do it ("run this report every day at 9am"), and it "wakes up" at that time to execute, then either goes back to sleep (recurring) or shuts off completely (one-time). No need to keep checking — it handles itself.
Scheduled Tasks = cron for Claude Code: run prompts automatically at specific times, no prompting/babysitting needed
Uses OS native schedulers: macOS (launchd), Linux (crontab), Windows (Task Scheduler)
Can access files, skills, tools, browser extensions like any Cowork task
One-time tasks auto-delete after execution; recurring tasks continue on schedule
Different from Loops: Loops run continuously, Scheduled Tasks run at specific times
Use cases: daily reports, monitoring, backups, weekly summaries, one-time reminders
Detection: Unless "every"/"daily"/"weekly" keywords present → treated as one-time

One-time tasks pin fire time to specific minute/hour using cron expression, confirm when it will fire, and delete themselves after running once — fully autonomous self-healing AI agents that run on schedule without supervision.
When working with cron/scheduled agents (claude scheduled tasks pattern), following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with cron/scheduled agents (claude scheduled tasks pattern). Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of cron/scheduled agents (claude scheduled tasks pattern) scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
